health_history.asthma_explain: I have never been diagnosed with asthma and have never required the use of an inhaler or any asthma-specific medications. I regularly participate in moderate to strenuous outdoor activities such as hiking, backpacking, and playing recreational basketball without any wheezing, chest tightness, or breathing difficulties that would suggest asthma. No primary care provider or urgent care clinician has ever raised a concern about asthma in my medical history.
health_history.diabetes_explain: I have never been diagnosed with Type 1 or Type 2 diabetes, nor have I had gestational diabetes (not applicable as I am male). I have had routine blood work done as part of annual physicals, and my fasting glucose has consistently been within the normal range, according to my primary care physician. I monitor my diet reasonably closely because of family history of Type 2 diabetes in older relatives, but I have never required blood sugar medication, insulin, or any formal diabetic counseling. I have never had symptoms such as unexplained excessive thirst, frequent urination, or unexplained weight loss that would suggest diabetes.
health_history.hypertension_explain:
I was diagnosed with mild essential hypertension in my late 30s during a routine physical exam when my blood pressure readings were consistently around 140/90 mmHg on multiple visits. My primary care physician started me on a low-dose ACE inhibitor (lisinopril 10 mg once daily) approximately five years ago. Since starting medication and improving my diet and exercise routine, my blood pressure has been well controlled, typically in the range of 120–130/75–85 mmHg at home and in the clinic.

I monitor my blood pressure at home about once a week using an automatic cuff and bring the log to my annual check-ups. I have never had a hypertensive emergency, chest pain related to blood pressure, or any hospitalizations for blood pressure issues. I do not experience side effects from the medication aside from a very mild dry cough when I first started it, which resolved on its own. My physician is aware that I participate in outdoor activities, including hiking and camping, and has cleared me for these activities as long as I stay hydrated, take my medication as prescribed, and pay attention to any unusual symptoms such as severe headaches, chest pain, or shortness of breath.

I take my medication consistently in the morning with breakfast and carry a small weekly pill case with me on trips to ensure I do not miss doses.
health_history.heart_disease_heart_attack_chest_pain_he:
I have no history of coronary artery disease, heart attack, heart failure, or structural heart problems. I have never been told I have a heart murmur, and no provider has heard any abnormal heart sounds during routine physical exams. I have never had unexplained chest pain during rest or exertion.

Because of my family history of high blood pressure and high cholesterol, my primary care physician ordered an EKG and basic cardiac workup about three years ago during a comprehensive physical. The EKG was normal, and my cholesterol, while slightly elevated at that time, has since improved with diet and exercise. I have never required any heart-related medications other than my blood pressure medication, and I have never seen a cardiologist as a patient. I can hike several miles with a backpack and climb moderate hills without chest discomfort or unusual shortness of breath beyond normal exertion.
health_history.stroke_tia_explain: I have never experienced a stroke or transient ischemic attack (TIA). I have never had sudden weakness, facial drooping, speech difficulties, or loss of coordination that required emergency evaluation for stroke. I have not been prescribed any blood thinners such as warfarin, apixaban, or clopidogrel for stroke prevention. My only cardiovascular risk factor is mild, well-controlled hypertension, and I manage this with medication, regular exercise, weight management, and routine follow-ups with my primary care physician.
health_history.lung_respiratory_disease_explain:
I have no history of chronic lung or respiratory diseases such as COPD, chronic bronchitis, emphysema, or interstitial lung disease. I have never required home oxygen, nebulizer treatments, or long-term inhaler use. I am a lifelong non-smoker and have not had significant secondhand smoke exposure at home or at work.

I do occasionally get typical seasonal upper respiratory infections (e.g., common colds) once or twice a year, which resolve on their own with rest, fluids, and over-the-counter medications. I have not had pneumonia or bronchitis as an adult. I am able to hike at altitude (up to around 6,000–7,000 feet) without persistent shortness of breath and have not been told of any abnormal lung findings during physical exams.
health_history.ear_sinus_problems_explain:
I have a history of mild seasonal allergic rhinitis and sinus congestion, especially in the spring and early fall when pollen counts are high. Symptoms include nasal congestion, post-nasal drip, and occasional sinus pressure headaches. I manage this with over-the-counter antihistamines such as cetirizine (Zyrtec) and a saline nasal rinse as needed. On rare occasions, I use a fluticasone nasal spray during peak allergy season if my symptoms are more noticeable.

These sinus issues are intermittent and generally mild. They have never caused significant breathing difficulty, ear infections, or hearing problems. I have not required sinus surgery or ENT specialist care. When camping or outdoors, I sometimes bring a small supply of antihistamines and a nasal spray in case pollen is high, but I am fully functional and can participate in all planned activities.
health_history.muscular_skeletal_condition_explain:
I have mild chronic low back stiffness and occasional discomfort, which my primary care physician attributes to a combination of an old minor sports strain and general deconditioning from years of desk work. About eight years ago, I strained my lower back while lifting a heavy box incorrectly. I did not require surgery or hospitalization, but I had several weeks of physical therapy focused on core strengthening and proper lifting techniques.

Since then, I experience intermittent lower back tightness, especially after long car rides or if I sleep on a very soft mattress. I manage this with regular stretching, core-strengthening exercises, and mindful lifting techniques. When I am on camping or hiking trips, I am careful not to overload my pack and to bend at the knees when lifting gear. I occasionally take over-the-counter ibuprofen for soreness after long days of activity but do not need it on a daily basis. My back condition does not limit my ability to hike, camp, or participate in regular troop activities, as long as I pace myself and avoid sudden heavy lifting without assistance.

I have no history of broken bones that required surgery, no joint replacements, and no diagnosed arthritis, although I do sometimes notice mild knee stiffness after long hikes on steep descents. That stiffness resolves with rest, stretching, and proper footwear.
health_history.psychiatric_psychological_and_emotional_:
I have not been diagnosed with any psychiatric or psychological disorders such as major depression, bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, or anxiety disorders. I have not taken prescription medications for mental health conditions, and I have never been hospitalized for psychiatric reasons.

Like many people, I experience normal life stress related to work, parenting, and general responsibilities. I manage this through regular exercise (walking, hiking, and light strength training), prayer and church involvement, talking openly with my spouse, and maintaining a reasonably consistent sleep schedule. I have not had episodes of panic attacks, suicidal thoughts, or self-harm behaviors. I am generally even-tempered and able to function reliably in group settings, including overnight trips and extended outdoor activities.
health_history.behavioral_neurological_disorders_explai:
I have no history of behavioral or neurological disorders such as ADHD, autism spectrum disorder, Tourette syndrome, Parkinson’s disease, multiple sclerosis, or other significant neurologic conditions. I have never experienced unexplained episodes of loss of consciousness (other than simple fainting as noted elsewhere if applicable—none in my case), sudden weakness, or persistent numbness.

I have not required evaluation by a neurologist, and I have not undergone brain imaging such as CT or MRI for neurologic symptoms. My coordination, balance, and gait are normal. I am able to safely hike on uneven terrain, handle tools, and supervise youth without neurological limitations.
health_history.bleeding_disorders_explain:
I do not have any known bleeding or clotting disorders such as hemophilia, von Willebrand disease, or a history of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) or pulmonary embolism. I do not bruise excessively, and I have not had prolonged bleeding after minor cuts, dental work, or previous medical procedures.

I am not on any blood-thinning medications like warfarin, apixaban, rivaroxaban, or daily prescription-strength aspirin. In the past, I have used over-the-counter ibuprofen for occasional pain or headaches without experiencing unusual bleeding issues such as nosebleeds or gastrointestinal bleeding.
health_history.fainting_spells_explain:
I have never had recurrent fainting spells or unexplained episodes of passing out. I have not experienced dizziness severe enough to cause loss of consciousness. On rare occasions, if I stand up too quickly after sitting for a long time, I may feel briefly lightheaded for a second or two, but this resolves immediately and has never led to a fall or fainting.

I have not required evaluation by a cardiologist or neurologist for syncope, and I do not take any medications known to cause frequent fainting. I am able to participate fully in physically demanding activities without episodes of blacking out or near-syncope.
health_history.thyroid_disease_explain:
I have never been diagnosed with hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, or any structural thyroid disease such as thyroid nodules or goiter. Routine blood tests, including thyroid function tests (TSH), have been within normal limits when checked during annual physicals at the recommendation of my primary care physician.

I do not take thyroid medications (such as levothyroxine or antithyroid drugs), and I have not experienced symptoms commonly associated with thyroid disease, such as unexplained significant weight changes, persistent fatigue beyond normal, heat or cold intolerance, or hair loss that would prompt further evaluation.
health_history.kidney_disease_explain:
I have no history of acute or chronic kidney disease. My kidney function labs (creatinine, BUN, and estimated GFR) have been within normal ranges during my routine blood work. I have not had kidney stones, blood in my urine, or recurrent urinary tract infections.

I stay hydrated, especially during outdoor activities, and I am mindful about using over-the-counter pain relievers like ibuprofen in moderation to avoid unnecessary strain on my kidneys. I do not require any dietary restrictions related to kidney function, such as low potassium or low phosphorus diets.
health_history.sickle_cell_disease_explain:
I do not have sickle cell disease or sickle cell trait to my knowledge. There is no known family history of sickle cell disease. I have never experienced sickle-cell-related pain crises, episodes of unexplained severe bone or chest pain, or hospitalizations related to anemia or sickle complications.

Routine blood work has never indicated abnormal red blood cell indices suggestive of sickle cell disease, and no provider has recommended specific testing based on my history or ethnic background.
health_history.seizures_explain:
I have never had a seizure, including generalized tonic-clonic seizures or partial/absence seizures. I have not experienced unexplained episodes of lost time, unresponsiveness, or involuntary movements that would suggest seizure activity.

I have not been evaluated by a neurologist for seizures, have never had an EEG, and have never taken anti-seizure medications such as levetiracetam, lamotrigine, or valproate. I am safe to participate in activities such as swimming, hiking on uneven terrain, and supervising youth without concern for seizure-related safety issues.
health_history.sleep_disorders_eg_sleep_walking_sleep_a:
I have not been diagnosed with any formal sleep disorders such as obstructive sleep apnea, narcolepsy, or chronic insomnia. My spouse has not observed loud, persistent snoring with pauses in breathing that would suggest sleep apnea, and I generally wake up feeling reasonably rested when I maintain a consistent sleep schedule.

I do not sleepwalk or engage in complex behaviors during sleep. On camping trips, I typically sleep a bit more lightly than at home due to outdoor noises, but I still get enough rest to function well the next day. I do not use prescription sleep medications, and I only occasionally use over-the-counter melatonin for long travel days or time-zone changes.
health_history.abdominal_digestive_problems_explain:
I have a history of mild gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), especially when I eat late at night or consume spicy or highly acidic foods. Symptoms include a burning sensation in the chest and upper abdomen and occasional sour taste in the mouth, usually occurring in the evening or when lying down shortly after a heavy meal.

To manage this, I try to avoid large, late meals and limit trigger foods like very spicy chili, tomato-heavy dishes, and large amounts of coffee. When symptoms do occur, I typically take an over-the-counter antacid (such as Tums) or, on rare occasions, a short course of an H2 blocker like famotidine. I have not required daily prescription medication for GERD, and I have not had complications such as ulcers, GI bleeding, or difficulty swallowing.

During camping or outdoor events, I try to eat a bit earlier in the evening and avoid lying flat immediately after a big meal. I also keep a small travel-sized bottle of antacids in my toiletry bag as a precaution. The reflux is generally mild and does not interfere with my ability to participate fully in activities.
health_history.surgery_explain:
I have had two notable surgical procedures:

1) Appendectomy (removal of appendix): At age 19, I developed acute appendicitis and underwent a laparoscopic appendectomy. The surgery was performed without complications, and I was discharged from the hospital the next day. Recovery took a couple of weeks, during which I avoided heavy lifting and strenuous exercise. I have had no long-term digestive issues or complications related to this surgery.

2) Right inguinal hernia repair: At age 34, I developed a right inguinal hernia, likely related to lifting and physical work. I had an elective outpatient surgical repair with mesh. The surgery and recovery were straightforward. I followed the surgeon’s instructions to avoid heavy lifting for several weeks. Since then, I have had no recurrence of the hernia, no chronic pain, and no restrictions on physical activity.

Both surgeries healed well, and I do not currently require any special accommodations or limitations due to these past procedures.
health_history.serious_injury_explain:
I have not experienced any serious injuries that required hospitalization, surgery beyond what is described above, or long-term rehabilitation. I have had typical minor injuries over the years—such as ankle sprains from recreational basketball and small cuts or bruises from yard work—but these have all resolved without lasting effects.

I have no history of concussions with prolonged symptoms, no major fractures requiring hardware (plates or screws), and no spinal injuries. I am able to walk, hike, and carry a backpack without significant limitations beyond normal fatigue and the usual minor aches that come with age and activity.
health_history.excessive_fatigue_or_shortness_of_breath:
I do not experience excessive fatigue or unusual shortness of breath with routine exercise for my age and fitness level. When hiking on steep inclines or carrying a moderately heavy backpack, I get winded in a way that feels appropriate for the effort, and my breathing returns to normal quickly when I rest.

I regularly walk 2–3 miles several times a week and participate in weekend hikes with my family or troop. I am able to complete these activities without needing to stop frequently due to breathing problems or extreme tiredness. I have never had to discontinue a hike or group activity because of unexplained shortness of breath or fatigue.

My primary care physician is aware of my activity level and has not expressed any concerns about my ability to safely participate in outdoor programs, assuming I stay hydrated, pace myself, and listen to my body when it comes to rest and recovery.
health_history.other_explain:
I do not have any other significant medical conditions that have not already been described above. I have no known drug allergies and no food allergies beyond mild seasonal environmental allergies as described. I am up to date on routine immunizations per my primary care physician’s recommendations, including tetanus and annual influenza vaccination.

I wear corrective lenses (glasses) for mild nearsightedness, especially when driving or reading small print at a distance, but this does not impact my ability to participate in outdoor activities as long as I have my glasses with me. I do not require any special medical equipment for daily life or for participation in camping or hiking activities.
